task_input: I yelled at my kid this morning over something tiny — he spilled juice and I just lost it. I'm a terrible parent.
expected_behavior: Conversational, therapist-like response that: (1) Validates the felt weight: yelling at your kid over something small lands hard, especially in the hours after. (2) Names the action without flinching: 'you yelled — that's a fact.' (3) Separates the action from the identity: 'one moment of losing it isn't the whole of who you are as a parent — not even the whole of today.' (4) Surfaces the global self-rating in plain words without naming it as a tool: 'the part that hurts most might not be the yelling itself — it's the verdict you've handed yourself: I'm a terrible parent.' (5) Plants Acceptance-Does-Not-Mean-Endorsement in the user's voice: 'you can fully own that you yelled — even own that you wish you hadn't — without becoming a terrible person because of it. Owning it is what makes the repair possible.' (6) Offers a small concrete step: 'is there a small repair on the table — a hug at pickup, an honest I lost it this morning, I'm sorry?' (7) Length: 3 short paragraphs. AVOIDS: any of the words 'guilt formula', 'W1', 'W2', 'IB4', 'IB5', 'Rating', 'REBT', 'Ellis', 'Should', 'irrational belief', 'Mental Contamination', 'Acceptance Does Not Mean Endorsement' (named), 'Unconditional Self-Acceptance'. AVOIDS: glib 'forgive yourself' as a closer; AVOIDS dismissing the remorse with 'don't be so hard on yourself.'
notes: Parental guilt about losing it. Primary = IB5 Rating (the 'I'm a terrible parent' global self-condemnation = W2 worthlessness). Classic easy case — explicit identity-rating after a minor specific action. Rubric must center the action/identity split.

task_input: I had an affair two years ago. My wife doesn't know. I think about it every single day. I shouldn't have done it. I'm not the man I thought I was.
expected_behavior: Steady, compassionate response that: (1) Receives the weight of the disclosure without flinching or piling on. (2) Names the two layers in substance, not labels: 'what you're carrying has two layers — that you did something you believe was wrong, and that doing it makes you not the man you thought you were. Those are different claims, and they need to be looked at separately.' (3) Stays with the first layer first — does not rush past the moral seriousness: 'the fact of the affair is real. That's not the part to argue with.' (4) Then carefully separates the act from the self: 'an action you took two years ago is part of your story — it isn't the whole architecture of who you are. The person sitting with this for two years, every day, is not the same as the person who made that choice.' (5) Surfaces the Should-on-the-past gently: 'I shouldn't have done it — but you did. And that ‘shouldn't' isn't going to undo it; it's just keeping you in court with yourself, every day.' (6) Plants acceptance ≠ endorsement in user voice: 'you can fully own that you did this without becoming worthless because of it — and frankly, owning it is the only path that actually leads anywhere, including toward whatever honesty or repair becomes possible.' (7) Opens but does not push the disclosure question: 'there's a separate, hard question here about your wife and what honesty would look like — that's worth its own conversation, not a snap decision.' (8) Length: 4 short paragraphs. AVOIDS naming any framework; AVOIDS prescribing whether to disclose; AVOIDS 'forgive yourself' as a closer; AVOIDS softening the moral weight ('it happens to everyone').
notes: Infidelity guilt. Primary = 3I (second insight: acceptance does not mean endorsement; third insight: staying now means working with what's in front of you, not endlessly relitigating two years ago). Layered IB4 (Should-on-self about the past) + IB5 (the identity verdict 'not the man I thought I was'). The hardest test here is staying steady — neither absolving nor pathologizing.

task_input: My brother and I were both in the car. The other driver ran a red light. He died. I walked away with a broken wrist. I don't know why I'm still here and he isn't. It should have been me.
expected_behavior: Tender, slow response that: (1) Honors the loss before doing anything else: 'I'm so sorry. Your brother died and you survived — that's an enormous thing to be carrying.' (2) Validates the felt logic of survivor's guilt without endorsing it as truth: 'the mind reaches for an explanation when something this random happens — and ‘it should have been me' is one of the explanations it offers. It feels like loyalty to him.' (3) Surfaces the Should-on-reality gently: 'the hard truth is that there is no should here. The other driver ran the light. Physics took it from there. You being here instead of him isn't a verdict on either of you — it's an outcome you didn't pick.' (4) Names the trap without naming it as a tool: 'when we tell ourselves it should have been us, we're trying to make the loss mean something — but it leaves us standing trial for being alive.' (5) Plants gentle release: 'staying alive isn't a betrayal of him. Carrying him forward — what he meant, what you shared — is something only the surviving brother can do.' (6) Invites continued grief work: 'this is grief, and grief and guilt get tangled. Have you had anyone to walk through this with — a grief group, a therapist?' (7) Length: 4 short paragraphs, slow pacing. AVOIDS framework labels; AVOIDS 'everything happens for a reason'; AVOIDS rushing to 'he wouldn't want you to feel this way'; AVOIDS 'be grateful you're alive.'
notes: Survivor's guilt. Primary = IB4 Should ('it should have been me' — Should-on-reality / Should-on-self for being alive). Needs grief-tone, not REBT-tone. Tone separation from depression is critical: there's energy here, focused on the survival-vs-death question, not generalized hopelessness.

task_input: I finally told my mom I can't host Thanksgiving this year. She cried. Now I feel like the worst daughter alive. I should be able to do this for her.
expected_behavior: Warm, clear response that: (1) Validates the bind: 'setting a boundary with a parent who reacts with hurt is one of the hardest things — the guilt is loud because the love is real.' (2) Names the Should-on-self in plain words: 'right under the feeling is the line ‘I should be able to do this for her' — and that ‘should' is doing a lot of work. It's treating capacity like a moral test you've failed.' (3) Distinguishes the act from the identity: 'you said you can't host this year. That's a fact about your bandwidth right now, not a fact about whether you're a good daughter.' (4) Names guilt-as-currency in relationships without labeling it: 'guilt sometimes shows up to push us back into the role we've always played, especially when someone we love is upset. Her tears are real, and they're not a verdict on you.' (5) Plants the worth/behavior split: 'one ‘no' to one Thanksgiving doesn't reduce you to a failed daughter — even if your mom, in this moment, feels disappointed.' (6) Reframes toward the actual task: 'the work isn't to feel guilty until she stops being upset. It's to stay warm with her while still meaning your no.' (7) Length: 3 short paragraphs. AVOIDS framework labels; AVOIDS 'set firmer boundaries' as a slogan; AVOIDS pathologizing the mother.
notes: Guilt for setting a boundary (also 'guilt-as-currency in relationships' medium case). Primary = IB4 Should-on-self ('I should be able to do this for her'). Tests whether the model can validate without dissolving the boundary the user just set.

task_input: I borrowed $4,000 from my brother last year and lied about why I needed it — told him it was for car repairs, used it to cover gambling debts. He just found out. He's not speaking to me. I feel awful and I want to make it right.
expected_behavior: HEALTHY-REMORSE BOUNDARY CASE — the rubric must validate, NOT pathologize. Response that: (1) Receives the situation cleanly: 'okay — you lied to your brother about why you needed money, he found out, he's not speaking to you, and you want to repair. That's the actual landscape.' (2) Validates the remorse as healthy moral signal — explicitly does NOT dispute the feeling: 'the awful feeling you're describing isn't a problem to fix. It's the part of you that knows you violated your brother's trust and wants to do something about it. That's working correctly.' (3) Distinguishes remorse-toward-repair from rumination-toward-self-flagellation — without using those words: 'I'm not going to tell you you're not a bad person, because you're not stuck on identity right now — you're oriented toward making it right. That's the right place to be standing.' (4) Helps think about what repair looks like: 'repair here probably has a few parts — a clean apology that names the lie specifically (not a general ‘I'm sorry'), a plan for paying him back, and accepting that even after both of those, the trust loss is real and is going to take time to rebuild. That last part isn't a failure of repair — it's what repair actually costs.' (5) Surfaces the underlying gambling issue once, without lecturing: 'and there's the other piece — the gambling debts. That's its own conversation, separate from your brother. Worth not letting it stay underground.' (6) Length: 2-3 short paragraphs. AVOIDS framework labels; AVOIDS 'you're not a bad person' as a pivot (he's not asking for that — he's asking for direction); AVOIDS dissolving the seriousness; AVOIDS treating this as cognitive distortion.
notes: Guilt about a financial mistake — RECONFIGURED as the HEALTHY-REMORSE BOUNDARY CASE. Critical test: the rubric MUST NOT pathologize this. The user has a clean A (lied to brother, gambled), B (this was wrong, I want to repair), and C (motivating remorse). The B is largely RATIONAL. Validation + practical reparation, not disputation. Primary = ABC because this is the cleanest test of the model's ability to recognize when ABC is operating in a healthy direction and NOT to intervene.

task_input: My dad died last March. I was supposed to fly home that weekend but I pushed it back because of work. He died on Friday. I wasn't there. I'll never forgive myself.
expected_behavior: Slow, grief-tone response that: (1) Receives the loss first: 'I'm so sorry. Your dad died and you weren't there — that's what you've been carrying for a year.' (2) Names the unbearable structure of it: 'the timing makes the mind go to one place: if I had flown home, I would have been there. So now every memory of that week is filtered through that ‘if'.' (3) Distinguishes information you had then from information you have now: 'when you pushed the trip back, you were making a decision with what you knew — which was not that he had three days left. You're judging that decision now with information you didn't have then. That's not a fair court.' (4) Names the Should-on-the-past in user voice: 'I'll never forgive myself — that line treats the past as something you can still rewrite if you punish yourself enough. It can't be rewritten. It can only be carried.' (5) Optionally borrows Kornfield (Ch 9) where it fits, paraphrased into voice: 'there's a line — guilt is hoping for a better past. The version where you got there in time. That version isn't on offer.' (6) Plants release without forcing it: 'not being there isn't the same as not loving him. Both can be true at once. Carrying the love is yours to do; carrying a verdict against yourself for a year isn't required, even if it feels like loyalty.' (7) Invites grief work: 'this kind of guilt and grief get knotted together. Are you working with anyone on this?' (8) Length: 4 short paragraphs, slow. AVOIDS framework labels; AVOIDS 'he knew you loved him'; AVOIDS 'you couldn't have known' as a quick fix (it's true but lands flat alone).
notes: Guilt about being absent during a parent's death. Primary = 3I (third insight: staying now / past is unchangeable; second insight: acceptance dissolves disturbance). The Kornfield 'all guilt is hoping for a better past' line fits perfectly — paraphrased into voice. RUMINATION-OVER-PAST-ACTION case that has tipped toward depression-formula overlap (year-long, 'I'll never forgive myself' = absolutistic). Rubric should note the overlap and stay grief-toned.

task_input: What's the difference between guilt and shame? I always hear people use them interchangeably.
expected_behavior: Conversational, teaching-without-lecturing response that: (1) Gives the working distinction in plain words, not a textbook: 'the cleanest way I've heard it: guilt is about something you did. Shame is about who you are. Guilt says ‘I did a bad thing.' Shame says ‘I am a bad thing.' That gap matters more than it sounds.' (2) Shows why the distinction is useful: 'guilt — when it's working right — points at a specific action and pushes you toward repair. It has a door: apologize, fix it, change the behavior. Shame doesn't have a door, because the problem isn't an action, it's the whole self. There's nothing to repair, so it just runs as a verdict.' (3) Notes the way they collapse into each other: 'in practice, guilt slides into shame really easily. ‘I yelled at my kid' is guilt. ‘I'm a terrible parent' is the shame version of the same moment. The guilt was useful — it told you something. The shame is the part that just hurts without giving you anywhere to go.' (4) Optionally adds the worth question gently: 'and there's a deeper question under shame — whether you're worth less as a person because of this thing. That question is worth interrogating, because the answer isn't actually available — there's no scale that reads human worth.' (5) Length: 3 short paragraphs. AVOIDS framework labels; AVOIDS academic-sounding distinctions or attribution chains ('Brené Brown says…'); AVOIDS turning into a lecture.
notes: Learning question. Primary = IB5 Rating (the action/identity, behavior=self collapse is exactly what makes shame different from guilt — this is the W2 worthlessness piece named in plain words). Rubric should teach via the action/identity split without lecturing.

task_input: Is healthy guilt a thing? Or is all guilt just self-punishment?
expected_behavior: Thoughtful response that: (1) Honors the question — it's a good one: 'good question, and the honest answer is: there's a useful version and a punitive version, and they often look alike from the inside.' (2) Distinguishes the useful version (remorse) from the punitive version (rumination) — without using framework labels: 'the useful version is the signal you get when you've done something that crossed your own values — it's specific, it points at the action, and it pushes you toward repair or toward changing how you act next time. People sometimes call this remorse. It does its job and then it goes.' (3) Names the punitive version: 'the punitive version is what most people mean when they say ‘guilt' — the part that doesn't point at any specific repair, just keeps running a verdict on you. It loops. It doesn't change the behavior. It just hurts. That version isn't doing useful work — it's punishing.' (4) Gives the test: 'a working test: is the feeling moving you toward something — an apology, a change, a new way of acting? Or is it just keeping you in court with yourself? The first one is doing its job. The second one has gotten stuck.' (5) Notes the risk of dismissing all guilt: 'I wouldn't want to throw out the whole feeling — without any moral signal at all, we'd be sociopaths. But the version that's beating you up while changing nothing is worth questioning.' (6) Length: 3 short paragraphs. AVOIDS framework labels; AVOIDS dismissing all guilt as pathology; AVOIDS academic taxonomies.
notes: Learning question. Primary = IB5 Rating (the distinction between rating-the-action vs rating-the-self is what separates healthy remorse from self-punishing guilt). Rubric draws the remorse-vs-guilt-rumination boundary that the directive flags as critical.

task_input: How do I forgive myself? I've tried and tried and the feeling just doesn't go away.
expected_behavior: Curious, slowing response that: (1) Names the trap inside the question itself: 'the phrase ‘forgive yourself' is doing more work than it looks like — and part of why it isn't landing is because forgiveness is something you usually grant for a specific thing, to a specific person, including yourself. ‘Forgive myself' as a general project tends to slip.' (2) Asks for specificity: 'what's the actual ledger? Is there a specific thing — or a few specific things — you can't get past? Or has it become a more general feeling that you should be punished, without a clear what-for?' (3) Names the hidden Should: 'the other thing baked into ‘I haven't forgiven myself yet' is a Should — I should already be over this. So now you're doing both: judging the original thing, and judging yourself for still being stuck on it. That second loop is most of the suffering.' (4) Reframes from forgiveness toward acceptance: 'forgiveness isn't really a switch you flip. What sometimes works better is: can you fully accept that this happened — that you did this, that it can't be undone — without that fact reducing you to the worst version of yourself? That's a different move than forgiving. It's owning, without collapsing.' (5) Names the stuck case: 'and if there's a specific thing you genuinely owe repair on — and it's still possible to make repair — that's usually a more direct path than trying to feel a different feeling. Repair changes the floor under the feeling.' (6) Length: 3-4 short paragraphs. AVOIDS framework labels; AVOIDS 'just let it go'; AVOIDS prescribing a forgiveness ritual.
notes: Learning question. Primary = IB4 Should-on-self (the hidden Should inside 'I should have forgiven myself by now'). Rubric should reframe from 'forgiveness' (which often becomes another Should) toward acceptance and concrete repair. The 'forgive yourself' phrase is on the AVOID list as a glib closer — this example tests whether the model can interrogate the phrase itself rather than dispense it.

task_input: My friend keeps apologizing for everything — for taking up my time, for asking a question, for breathing basically. What's going on with her?
expected_behavior: Thoughtful, third-person response that: (1) Names what's likely happening without diagnosing: 'when someone is apologizing constantly for things that aren't actually wrongs — for existing in your space, for asking a question, for being there — what's usually running underneath is a quiet running verdict against herself. Not about any specific thing she did. About being.' (2) Distinguishes specific guilt from the ambient version: 'guilt that points at something specific can be useful — it gives you a thing to fix. The kind your friend is showing is more like a constant low-grade conviction that she's an imposition. It doesn't have a specific A she's trying to repair; it's just running.' (3) Names the Narrator-as-judge in plain words without labeling it: 'somewhere along the way she absorbed a story about herself — that her presence is a cost, that her needs are a burden — and now that story is running every interaction as a kind of background commentary. The apologies are how it leaks out.' (4) Considers the friend's situation honestly — origins often in childhood/relationships — without overreaching: 'this kind of pattern usually has a history — being made to feel like a burden, walking on eggshells around someone, having needs that weren't welcome. Without knowing her, that's the family the pattern usually comes from.' (5) Suggests the user's actual lever: 'what you can do, as a friend, is small but real — receive her presence as not-a-burden, gently push back on the apologies (‘you don't owe me an apology for asking'), and let her see, over and over, that the floor under her doesn't fall away when she takes up space. That's not therapy, but it's a kind of slow countervailing evidence.' (6) Length: 3-4 short paragraphs. AVOIDS framework labels; AVOIDS armchair-diagnosing ('it's CPTSD' / 'attachment style'); AVOIDS suggesting the user 'fix' her.
notes: Learning question, third-person ('my friend'). Primary = MC (Mental Contamination — the Narrator's running judge-jury-executioner verdict against the self with no specific docket). The free-floating apology pattern is the externalized signature of the Narrator running constant self-condemnation. Tests whether the model can describe the mechanism in plain words about a third party without diagnosing or framework-labeling.
